tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'On a KVD 2.5, replace standard No.2 hooks with short shank No.1 around heavy cover for greater lip-grabbing ability.'
Hackney: 'Go to a 1/0 hook on the 2.5 to deaden the action. A shad does not have aggressive action. You still get the big profile but more natural movement for pressured fish.'
Hackney: 'Add a larger hook on the rear to make the bait tilt tail-down on the pause. In clear water those fish are looking at the bait and this will get them to commit.'
Hackney: 'Adding bigger hooks numbs the action enough for cold water fish. But you may need to drop line size to 10 or 8 pound to keep the bait neutral.'
Hackney: 'Adding a larger hook to the front end allows the jerkbait to reach a couple feet deeper without making it sink.'
Hackney: 'Upsize to 2X strong No.4 trebles on deep divers to swap buoyancy for a couple more feet of depth. The spots where you use a 6XD or 8XD don't have a lot of snags.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'I typically like to go with the biggest hook I can put on a treble hook bait because it increases my chances of catching a fish. A fish on a treble hook bait is harder to land than one caught on a single hook bait.'
Hackney: 'You can manipulate hooks because of water temperature. It is easier to sink a bait in cold water than warm water because air and gas inside the bait are less buoyant in cold water.'

Hackney: 'Adding No.4 2X strong trebles to a flat-sided balsa bait (Lucky Shad) tempers buoyancy and lets it suspend on contact with cover. It acts like a jerkbait but you can crank it and cover more water.'
Hackney: 'Only carry about 10 replacement hooks of each size. Overpacking trays leads to dull hooks as masses of metal bump against one another during long runs.'
